findings_summary.title: fast-json-stringify: JSON Injection via format:date-time String Passthrough
findings_summary.description: When a string (not Date object) is passed to a format:date-time/date/time schema field, the value is wrapped in double quotes with ZERO escaping. An attacker controlling the string value can inject arbitrary JSON structure, including overwriting other properties (last-value-wins in JSON.parse).
findings_summary.impact: Property injection, privilege escalation via role overwrite in downstream parsers

findings_summary.title: Content-based body schema dispatch bypass
findings_summary.description: When a route uses per-content-type body schemas and a request arrives with a Content-Type that has a registered parser but no matching body schema, the body is parsed but NOT validated. The handler receives an unvalidated body.
findings_summary.impact: Schema validation bypass for non-matching content types

findings_summary.title: fast-json-stringify: largeArrayMechanism bypasses all schema filtering
findings_summary.description: When largeArrayMechanism is set to json-stringify and array size exceeds largeArraySize (default 20000), the library falls back to JSON.stringify() for the entire array, completely bypassing all schema-based property filtering. Passwords, SSNs, API keys, etc. would be leaked.
findings_summary.impact: Complete information disclosure for array items above threshold

findings_summary.title: No timeout on request-lifecycle async hooks
findings_summary.description: hookRunnerGenerator has no timeout mechanism. A never-resolving Promise in an async hook holds the connection open indefinitely (until OS socket timeout). Multiple such requests could exhaust server connection capacity.
findings_summary.impact: Denial of service via connection exhaustion

findings_summary.title: URL-encoded form data passes JSON schema validation (CSRF vector)
findings_summary.description: When @fastify/formbody is registered, application/x-www-form-urlencoded bodies are parsed into flat objects that pass JSON schema validation. Since form-urlencoded is a CORS 'simple' content-type, cross-origin form submissions can invoke JSON-schema-validated routes without CORS preflight.
findings_summary.impact: CSRF on JSON-schema routes when @fastify/formbody is registered

findings_summary.title: fast-json-stringify: Empty schema {} leaks all nested data
findings_summary.description: Properties defined with empty schema {} (no type field) fall through to JSON.stringify(), serializing the entire value with all nested properties. Developers using {} as catch-all for flexible APIs inadvertently disable schema filtering for that subtree.
findings_summary.impact: Information disclosure for properties with empty schemas

findings_summary.title: Missing security response headers
findings_summary.description: Default configuration lacks CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection headers. No Server header (good for OPSEC).

findings_summary.title: Error messages can leak internal paths
findings_summary.description: FST_ERR_SCH_VALIDATION_BUILD includes method, URL, and internal error message. Stack traces are correctly stripped, but error messages may reveal server-side paths and configuration.

findings_summary.title: Prototype poisoning defaults to 'error' (safe default)
findings_summary.description: Default onProtoPoisoning='error' and onConstructorPoisoning='error'. Requests with __proto__ in JSON body are rejected with 400 error. This is the safest configuration.

findings_summary.title: CORS wildcard origin with credentials
findings_summary.description: CORS configured with origin:true + credentials:true reflects any Origin header. Common real-world misconfiguration but not a framework default.
